---
title: "The tau×amyloid microglial divergence: an integrated synthesis and current best conclusion"
subtitle: "Cross-arc reading of eleven evidence layers, four base modalities and a human translational test, grounded against the literature"
author: "Claude (autonomous agent)"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%d %B %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: !expr 'local({ source("R/report.R", local = TRUE); report_theme() })'
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: true
    df_print: paged
    fig_width: 9
    fig_height: 6
params:
  results_dir: "storage/results"
  figures_dir: "storage/figures"
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center", dev = "png", dpi = 110, cache = FALSE
)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath("."))
options(width = 110)

# This document is a read-only synthesis. It re-reads only the exported result
# tables in storage/results/ and embeds the pre-built capstone figure; it
# recomputes nothing, touches no heavy cache, and does not modify the locked
# analysis.Rmd section-17 ledger or its sealed contest margins.
rt <- function(f) read.delim(file.path(params$results_dir, f),
                             check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

# Overview {-}

This document states the best integrated conclusion the project currently
supports, synthesising eleven orthogonal evidence arcs, four base molecular
modalities and a human cross-species test into a single reading and grounding
each load-bearing claim against the external literature. The study crosses tau
(wild-type humanised *MAPT* versus P301S) with amyloid (App^NL-G-F^ knock-in
versus none) in a 2×2 factorial across four 24-month mouse genotypes, and asks
one question: does amyloid remodel hippocampal microglia *differently* depending
on the tau background? The central result is a negative finding with a
constructive resolution. Amyloid drives a strong, reproducible
disease-associated-microglia (DAM) programme that is the dominant signal in every
modality, whereas the tau×amyloid interaction, the project's core readout, is
null at the whole-microglia gene level and remains null under six
differential-expression engines, in every microglial substate, in every broad
cell type at matched power, and in tissue composition. The interaction has not
vanished; it has moved off the static gene axis and re-emerges as a coherent
*mechanistic* and *dynamic* signal: GSK3β kinase activation, Myc-led
transcriptional suppression, a recoverable kinase→transcription-factor causal
bridge, and a supra-additive acceleration of the homeostatic→DAM activation
trajectory. Two refinements complete the picture: on the amyloid background tau
*attenuates* rather than amplifies the amyloid-driven NF-κB response, and the
synaptic-suppression programme is dominated by TREM2/APP-fragment clearance
ligands rather than classical complement. The same structure is directionally
conserved in human Alzheimer cortex. The integrated model is therefore that
amyloid is the primary architect of the microglial response and tau is a
modifier that bends the *rate and signalling* of that response without
rewriting its differentially expressed gene set. This reading is then made
explicit as a single falsifiable systems model, ranked against four named
alternatives it must beat, and tied to the ordered set of experiments that
would decide between them.

# How the conclusion was assembled {-}

The reading below is a synthesis, not a re-analysis. Eight read-only agents
ingested every analysis arc, its rendered verdict prose and its exported result
tables, and five further agents grounded the headline claims in the current
neuroimmunology literature; the conclusions are then reconciled here. The locked
`analysis.Rmd` adjudication (its 92-row claims ledger, its eleven-entity
hypothesis set, its grade definitions and its three sealed contest margins) is
surfaced verbatim and is never re-opened. Where the external literature qualifies
an internal claim, the qualification is stated rather than smoothed away.

# The question and the design

The four genotypes form the corners of a tau-by-amyloid square: MAPTKI
(humanised wild-type tau, no amyloid), P301S (mutant tau, no amyloid),
NLGF_MAPTKI (amyloid on a wild-type-tau background) and NLGF_P301S (amyloid plus
mutant tau). The App^NL-G-F^ knock-in (Saito et al., 2014) accumulates amyloid
and provokes plaque-coupled microgliosis without the confounds of APP
overexpression, making it a clean amyloid driver. Each animal is read out across
single-nucleus RNA-seq (≈23,500 analysed microglia from a 286,000-nucleus
atlas), GeoMx spatial transcriptomics (91 microglia-enriched regions), and bulk
hippocampal proteomics and phosphoproteomics (16 samples, four replicates per
genotype). The divergence question is operationalised as a single interaction
contrast, the difference of the two amyloid effects across tau backgrounds:
(NLGF_P301S − P301S) − (NLGF_MAPTKI − MAPTKI). Pseudobulk replication uses
genotype×batch (16 units, four per genotype), with batch in the design.

The synthesis resolves into three biological axes (amyloid activation, synaptic
suppression and the tau×amyloid interaction), each adjudicated as a binary
contest between competing mechanistic hypotheses. The three sealed verdicts are
the spine of the conclusion.

```{r contest_table, results='asis'}
cs <- rt("capstone_contest_summary.tsv")
cap_lab <- c(amyloid_activation = "Amyloid activation",
             synaptic_suppression = "Synaptic suppression",
             interaction_metabolic = "Tau×amyloid interaction")
hyp_id  <- sub(" .*$", "", cs$winner)
hyp_dsc <- sub("^\\S+ \\((.*)\\)$", "\\1", cs$winner)
cs_disp <- data.frame(
  Axis            = cap_lab[cs$contest],
  `Favoured model`= sprintf("%s - %s", hyp_id, hyp_dsc),
  Margin          = cs$margin,
  `Strong/Mod/Sugg` = sprintf("%d / %d / %d", cs$n_strong, cs$n_moderate, cs$n_suggestive),
  Layers          = cs$n_layers,
  Modalities      = cs$n_modalities,
  check.names = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
cs_disp <- cs_disp[order(-cs$margin), ]
knitr::kable(
  cs_disp, row.names = FALSE, align = c("l", "l", "r", "c", "r", "r"),
  caption = paste0(
    "The three sealed contest verdicts (analysis report section 17), surfaced ",
    "read-only. Margin = favoured-model net support minus rival net support; ",
    "Strong/Mod/Sugg counts the grade of the supporting ledger rows; Layers and ",
    "Modalities count the distinct evidence layers and replicate modalities ",
    "behind the winner."
  )
)
```

# Amyloid activation: a DAM programme that tau restrains

## A reproducible disease-associated-microglia response

The amyloid axis is the strongest and most multi-layer-convergent finding in the
project, positive and at least moderately supported in nearly every layer from
expression through kinase activity, tissue composition and activation dynamics.
At the gene level, amyloid induces the canonical DAM signature on both tau
backgrounds: *Cst7* rises with a single-nucleus log~2~ fold-change of 4.72 (FDR
1.3×10⁻¹⁹) on the wild-type-tau background and 4.48 (FDR 4.0×10⁻²⁷) on the P301S
background, alongside *Colec12*, *Itgax*, *Lpl*, *Spp1*, *Trem2* and *Tyrobp*,
with the homeostatic markers *P2ry12* and *Tmem119* falling in step. This
programme is precisely the DAM state defined in the 5xFAD amyloid model
(Keren-Shaul et al., 2017) and independently as the APOE-TREM2-driven
"microglial neurodegenerative" (MGnD) phenotype (Krasemann et al., 2017;
Deczkowska et al., 2018), and the App^NL-G-F^ knock-in is an established model of
plaque-coupled microgliosis (Saito et al., 2014), so the breadth of this axis is
well-founded. The signal survives aggregation into co-expression modules, the
DAM-enriched hdWGCNA module (whose hub genes *Apoe*, *Cst7*, *Lpl*, *Spp1* and
*Tyrobp* overlap the canonical DAM set at odds ratio 6.0) is up across three to
four modalities at both amyloid contrasts, and the MHC-II/antigen-presentation
arm of the response (*Cd74*, GO adaptive-immune-response) recovers a formally
recognised fourth microglial state (Chen and Colonna, 2021). Transcription-factor
inference nominates a *Spi1*/*Nfkb1*/*Sp3* induction ensemble, and *Spi1* (PU.1)
is independently re-derived *de novo* by the data-driven SCENIC regulon analysis
(recovered in 10 of 10 runs), mirroring its status as the master microglial
lineage regulator and a causal Alzheimer risk locus (Huang et al., 2017).

The activation is corroborated by two evidence classes that read quantities no
expression analysis can. Spatial deconvolution of the GeoMx regions shows the DAM
tissue compartment expanding under amyloid (abundance log~2~ fold-change +0.40,
FDR 3.5×10⁻⁵, and +0.53, FDR 2.4×10⁻⁷, with the proliferative compartment its
mirror), the project's first cell-composition readout. Activation-trajectory
analysis shows amyloid advancing microglia along the homeostatic→DAM pseudotime
in both tau backgrounds (mean-pseudotime log~2~ fold-change +7.87, FDR
8.9×10⁻⁷, and +10.32, FDR 3.7×10⁻⁸; the fraction of cells past the DAM-onset
threshold rises from ≈4% to 40-59%). Critically, when the locked factorial DE is
re-run across six broad cell types at matched cell-count power, the amyloid main
effect remains microglia-led, 625 significant genes in microglia versus 218 in
the next-highest cell type, despite microglia carrying only ≈40% of the
neuronal transcriptome depth. The amyloid-driven DAM response is thus genuinely a
microglial specialty, robust to method, modality, aggregation and power.

## On the amyloid background, tau attenuates the NF-κB response

The amyloid contest is not won by the bare existence of the DAM programme (both
competing hypotheses concede that) but by *how tau modifies it*. The favoured
model (Hyp-1B, margin 18) is that tau attenuates the amyloid-driven NF-κB
response rather than amplifying it. Per-substate transcription-factor and
gene-set analysis shows the NF-κB-target signal reversing sign at the
interaction: the complex-level NF-κB regulon score is positive at both amyloid
contrasts but negative in all four substates at the interaction (consensus scores
−6.52 in DAM, −5.45 in homeostatic, −3.66 in IFN, −2.55 in proliferative), and
the NF-κB-target gene set is suppressed at the interaction in every substate (DAM
NES −1.25, FDR 0.027). A per-sample compositional ANOVA formally excludes a
substate-abundance artefact (tau:amyloid F = 1.03, p = 0.33), establishing the
attenuation as transcriptional and global rather than a Simpson's-paradox
shadow of the DAM expansion.

This runs against the dominant view that tau *amplifies* microglial NF-κB and
NLRP3 inflammation (Bhaskar et al., 2010; Ising et al., 2019; Wang et al., Nat.
Commun. 2022), but that evidence comes almost entirely from pure-tauopathy
models and speaks to the *main effect* of tau, not to how P301S modifies the
*amyloid*-driven response in a 2×2 design. Two independent lines support the
attenuation reading. Genetic tau reduction lowers microglial innate-immune output
and renders mice resistant to inflammatory neurodegeneration (Maphis et al.,
2015), and, most directly, an independent study in essentially the same
App^NL-G-F^/MAPT^P301S^ cross found the microglial and cytokine response to be
explicitly *non-additive*, with amyloid the dominant driver and tau adding little
(Jiang et al., 2024), the qPCR analogue of our transcriptome-wide sub-additive
NF-κB suppression. A plausible cell-biological route is that P301S tau drives
microglia toward a senescent, hypofunctional state (Brelstaff et al., 2021). The
honest caveat is that a sub-additive interaction is compatible with both
genotypes still being more inflammatory than tau-free baseline; we claim that tau
blunts the *amyloid increment*, not that tau is anti-inflammatory in absolute
terms, and a ceiling/saturation of the DAM-NF-κB programme under combined
pathology is a deflationary alternative that neither our design nor Jiang's can
fully exclude.

A second, important qualification concerns valence. The internal analysis labels
this axis "activation"; the literature cautions against reading activation as
pathology. PU.1 is *required* for the protective DAM phagocytic response, its
microglial deletion worsens amyloid burden (Yun et al., 2026) and a risk allele
that *lowers* PU.1 *delays* Alzheimer onset (Huang et al., 2017), and MHC-II⁺
microglia can *enhance* plaque clearance (Chen and Colonna, 2021). The amyloid
axis is therefore best read as a robust, predominantly compensatory DAM
mobilisation whose net protective-versus-pathogenic valence this dataset does not
resolve, rather than as a simple inflammatory-damage signal.

# Synaptic suppression: clearance-dominant, not complement-pruning

The synaptic-suppression axis is the project's most mechanistically surprising
result. Synaptic-compartment genes fall coherently at both amyloid contrasts, but
neither the transcription-factor nor the kinase layer names a distinct repressor,
an honest non-finding that points to an intercellular rather than a
regulator-driven mechanism. The ligand-receptor layer, run as a three-tool
triangulation (CellChat, MultiNicheNet and LIANA+), resolves it: the favoured
model (Hyp-2B, margin 12) is that suppression proceeds through up-regulated
TREM2/APP-fragment clearance ligands rather than classical complement. The DAM
clearance pairs *Apoe*-*Trem2* and *App*-*Cd74* rank near the top of the
microglia-receiver ordering (ranks 9 and 12) while the first classical-complement
pair *C1qb*-*Lrp1* ranks ≈775; nearly half of the top-100 axis pairs involve
microglia.

The external literature supports the *dichotomy* our finding draws. The
TREM2-APOE/DAM programme is the canonical microglial clearance axis (Krasemann et
al., 2017; Deczkowska et al., 2018), and TREM2 has been shown to bind the
complement initiator C1q directly and *antagonise* classical-complement
activation at the synapse, with TREM2 the protective arm (Zhong et al., 2023);
CD74 physically interacts with APP and suppresses Aβ production, giving the gained
*App*-*Cd74* pair specific biological plausibility (Bryan et al., 2009). The
caveat a reviewer would press hardest is that the two mechanisms are widely
regarded as complementary and stage-dependent rather than mutually exclusive: the
classical-complement synapse-pruning hypothesis is robust (Hong et al., 2016) and
C1q-driven, antibody-reversible synapse engulfment is documented specifically in
the P301S background that defines our interaction contrast (Dejanovic et al.,
2018). A low ligand-receptor rank for *C1qb*-*Lrp1* reflects expression-based
interaction scoring rather than functional pruning flux, and classical complement
acts through C1q→C3→CR3 (*Itgam*/*Itgb2*) opsonisation rather than the *C1qb*→
*Lrp1* pairing, so the chosen receptor may understate complement. The defensible
conclusion is therefore that the *up-regulated, dominant* axis is TREM2/APP
clearance: the finding is best stated as clearance-dominant, not
complement-absent.

# The tau×amyloid interaction: where the divergence lives

## Null on every static gene axis

The interaction is the project's core readout and its hardest result. At the
whole-microglia level it is null, and the null is exhaustively robust. Six
differential-expression engines spanning three shrinkage families agree: limma-
voom, edgeR, dream and single-cell NEBULA return zero genes at FDR < 0.05 for the
interaction, DESeq2 returns one and glmmTMB twenty-five, with the leading NEBULA
hit at FDR 0.063. The null holds within every microglial substate (zero genes in
all four), and, decisively, it holds in every broad cell type at matched power:
when the factorial is re-run across six cell types downsampled to equal cell
counts, the gene-level interaction collapses to zero in all of them including
microglia, the per-cell-type interaction profiles are mutually distinct
(microglia-versus-other-type Spearman 0.08-0.15) while the two estimators agree
almost perfectly (0.96-0.98). It is also null in tissue composition (DAM
abundance interaction +0.13, p = 0.33). The four apparent native-power microglial
interaction hits are exactly the artefact the matched-power control was built to
expose, and they vanish when power is equalised. This cross-cell-type analysis
reframes, without refuting, the project's microglial focus (the cross-cell-type
ledger entry, N-002, deliberately feeds no model): the amyloid main effect is
microglia-led, but the interaction is a tissue-wide property rather than a
microglial specialty. The methodological backbone for trusting a matched-power
null over the native-power hits is the demonstration that single-cell DE inflates
significance through pseudoreplication and that effective power scales with
replicate, not cell, count (Squair et al., 2021).

## Re-emergence as mechanism and as dynamics

What no static gene contrast can see, the mechanism and dynamics layers
recover, and they agree. The favoured model (Hyp-3B, margin 55, the largest in
the project and unanimously un-contradicted) is that the interaction has a
distinct synergy mechanism. Its single strongest result is the activation of
GSK3β, the canonical tau kinase, at the interaction contrast (consensus
activity +4.32, FDR 0.0015); the sign trajectory across the five contrasts
(−1.66, −1.47, +3.79, +4.15, +4.32) shows GSK3β suppressed by tau alone and by
amyloid alone but activated only by their combination, a textbook supra-additive
signature. In parallel, the biosynthetic/proliferation transcription factors Myc,
Creb1, Tp53 and Jun are coherently *suppressed* at the interaction (Myc mean
−3.25). The earlier "mixed-sign" label for this axis is resolved as a
modality-orthogonality artefact rather than a directional disagreement: the
kinase arm (tau-phosphorylation machinery on) and the transcription-factor arm
(biosynthetic throughput off) run in the same biological direction, and the
gene-set sign conflict came only from RNA-versus-phospho-corrected disagreement.
The causal-topology layer then supplies the wiring that the endpoint layers
lacked, recovering at the interaction a compact `Gsk3b ⊣ Mapk14 → Myc/Foxo3`
module that joins the project's lead kinase to its lead transcription factors
(all five interaction transcription factors recovered), the cleanest
single result of the causal arc and the join that unifies the synergy mechanism.

GSK3β is the textbook node where amyloid drives tau hyperphosphorylation and the
two pathologies converge (Hooper et al., 2008; Lauretti et al., 2020), with a
documented microglial NF-κB/pro-inflammatory arm (Zhao et al., 2024) that
connects this axis back to the amyloid-axis NF-κB result. Combined amyloid and
tau pathology is repeatedly reported to drive supra-additive rather than merely
additive microglial and transcriptional responses (Pickett et al., 2019; Kim et
al., 2022; Lee et al., 2021). Two honest qualifications apply. First, most
GSK3β-tau-phosphorylation evidence is neuronal, so framing GSK3β as a *microglial*
interaction effector leans on the matched-power specificity arc, which shows the
interaction is not microglia-restricted in any case. Second, the Myc-suppression
arm is the most inferential pillar: Myc is well established as a transient
early-proliferation and ribosome-biogenesis regulator in microglia (Wang et al.,
Cell Discov. 2022), so its suppression at a mature DAM-skewed interface is
consistent with, but not yet directly demonstrated as, an amyloid×tau signature.

The interaction's most distinctive positive signal is dynamic, not static. The
activation-trajectory analysis is the only layer in which the interaction is both
positive and significant: the P301S background supra-additively amplifies the
amyloid-driven advance along the homeostatic→DAM pseudotime (mean-pseudotime
interaction +2.46, FDR 0.077; fraction-past-threshold interaction +0.19, FDR
0.013). A progression-versus-composition decomposition, the load-bearing control,
shows this loads almost entirely on genuine within-state progression (≈94% of
the effect; composition channel +0.13, FDR 0.81, null), so it is non-redundant
with the composition layer's interaction null and cannot be a cell-mixing
artefact, since even homeostatic-labelled cells advance (within-homeostatic
interaction +2.68, FDR 0.077). A gene-level tradeSeq analysis of expression
dynamics along the trajectory then recovers 110 genes whose pseudotime *shape* is
tau×amyloid-modulated, none of which reach static matched-power significance,
handing the aggregate progression synergy a candidate gene-level signature that
static DE is blind to. This last result is reported with its caveats intact:
roughly a quarter of the 110 genes are ambient or ribosomal contaminants, and the
named Hyp-3B effectors GSK3β and Myc are *absent* from the set, so it corroborates
the existence of a dynamic interaction without naming its effectors and is
ledgered to feed no model.

This is where the project's reading must be reconciled with recent
combined-pathology studies that report amyloid×tau microglial *synergy*
explicitly (Kim et al., 2022; Coburn et al., 2025; Lu et al., 2026). The apparent
conflict with our gene-level null is largely a level-of-analysis distinction:
those studies measure synergy as state-abundance or programme induction, which
maps onto our retained progression-rate synergy, whereas our null is a
gene-level factorial contrast in 16 pseudobulk replicates, structurally
under-powered for an interaction term. The two readings are complementary, not
contradictory. The correct strength of claim is that there is no FDR-surviving
*gene-level* interaction under our power, while the interaction is real as a
progression-rate and signalling phenomenon, and is not microglia-restricted.

# The integrated model

```{r capstone_figure, out.width = "100%", fig.cap = "Integrated convergence of the tau×amyloid microglial model. Panel A (landscape) shows each evidence arc's own signed verdict per axis, with non-convergence first-class: grey null cells, the gold reframe cell where the specificity arc recast the interaction as not microglia-specific, and purple mixed cells where modalities disagree in sign. Panel B (contest) shows the three sealed section-17 margins. The human band is held separate and never counted in the mouse contests. Figure pre-built out-of-knit by scripts/build_capstone_figure.py."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(params$figures_dir, "capstone_convergence.png"))
```

Read together, the eleven arcs converge on one coherent mechanistic statement.
Amyloid is the primary architect of the microglial response: it mobilises a
predominantly compensatory DAM programme that is the dominant, most-replicated
signal across expression, pathway, transcription-factor, kinase, composition and
trajectory layers, and that remains microglia-led even at matched power. Tau is a
modifier acting on that amyloid-built response in two distinct registers. In the
*signalling* register, the combination of tau and amyloid supra-additively
activates GSK3β and suppresses Myc-led biosynthesis, wired through a recoverable
`Gsk3b ⊣ Mapk14 → Myc` topology; in the *dynamic* register, tau accelerates the
amyloid-driven progression of microglia toward the DAM state. Simultaneously, tau
*attenuates* the amyloid-driven NF-κB transcriptional response across all
substates, so its net effect is to bend the rate and the signalling of the
microglial response rather than to rewrite its differentially expressed gene set,
which is precisely why the interaction is null on every static gene axis yet
positive on the kinase, causal and trajectory axes. The synaptic consequence of
this remodelling is carried by up-regulated TREM2/APP-fragment clearance ligands.

The convergence matrix below makes the structure explicit. The amyloid column is
positive and convergent down nearly the whole table; the interaction column is
the synthesis's value-add, null on the static readouts (expression, composition,
specificity) but mechanism-signed (GSK3β up, Myc down) and dynamics-positive; the
synaptic column is moderate and clearance-flavoured. The later arcs (K-O) are
deliberately margin-neutral, each contributing only one or two ledger rows, so
they corroborate the decided contests orthogonally without moving them, which is
why the landscape view and the contest view are shown side by side.

```{r convergence_matrix, results='asis'}
cm <- rt("capstone_convergence_matrix.tsv")
axis_order <- c("amyloid_activation", "synaptic_suppression", "interaction_metabolic")
axis_lab   <- c(amyloid_activation = "Amyloid activation",
                synaptic_suppression = "Synaptic suppression",
                interaction_metabolic = "Tau×amyloid interaction")
cm$row <- sprintf("%s (%s)", cm$evidence_class, cm$arc)
rows <- unique(cm$row)
wide <- data.frame(`Evidence layer (arc)` = rows, check.names = FALSE)
for (ax in axis_order) {
  wide[[axis_lab[ax]]] <- cm$cell_value[match(paste(rows, ax), paste(cm$row, cm$axis))]
}
knitr::kable(
  wide, row.names = FALSE, align = c("l", "c", "c", "c"),
  caption = paste0(
    "Evidence-layer by biological-axis convergence matrix (each arc's own signed ",
    "verdict). Cell tokens combine sign and confidence: strong/mod/sugg = grade, ",
    "+/- = direction, mixed = modalities disagree in sign, null = tested but not ",
    "significant, reframes = recast the question (interaction is not ",
    "microglia-specific), n.a. = axis not probed by that arc. The final 'human' ",
    "row is a separate evidence band, directionally consistent with the mouse ",
    "mechanisms but never counted into the mouse contests."
  )
)
```

# Candidate holistic models: a generative layer above the contests

The synthesis so far has been an *adjudication*: each axis was settled as a binary
contest, and the section above distilled the winners into a single integrated
reading. This section takes the complementary, generative step. Where the contests
ask which hypothesis wins an axis, a holistic model asks what one coherent
biological *system* could produce all eleven arcs at once and, by committing to a
mechanism, exposes itself to falsification in a way a verdict tally does not. The
candidates below were produced by a dynamic multi-agent procedure that grounded
each mechanistic framework in the 2022-2026 neuroimmunology literature, generated
one unified systems model together with four deliberately distinct rivals, and then
subjected every model to adversarial critique against the project's own evidence
before reconciling them here. Three rules govern the exercise. The three sealed
section-17 verdicts are reinterpreted, never reopened. Each model is required to be
falsifiable by a concrete experiment and anchored to specific findings, so the set
can be *ranked* rather than merely listed. And because "holistic" invites reaching
past the adjudicated evidence (into feedback, temporal order, valence and
tissue-level organisation), every such reach is flagged as proposed and carries its
own literature anchor and falsification test. The lead is stated first, but its lead
is provisional; the rivals it must beat, and the experiments that would decide
between them, are given equal weight.

## The lead model: amyloid as architect, tau as a rate-and-wiring modifier

The favoured holistic reading formalises the integrated statement above into an
explicit, signed systems model (schematic below). Amyloid is the *architect*: it
templates the entire homeostatic→DAM activation trajectory to essentially the same
extent on either tau background (mean-pseudotime log~2~ fold-change +7.87 and
+10.32), mobilises the canonical DAM gene programme under the PU.1/Nfkb1/Sp3
ensemble, expands the DAM tissue compartment, and emits the TREM2/APOE and
APP-fragment clearance secretome that is the project's synaptic-axis output, a
response that remains microglia-led even at matched power. Mutant tau, by contrast,
does essentially nothing to this trajectory on its own. Tau is therefore not a
second architect but a *modifier* whose action is conditional on the amyloid
scaffold.

The modifier acts at a single integrating node. GSK3β kinase activity behaves as a
coincidence detector, a Boolean AND-gate, that is *suppressed* by tau alone and by
amyloid alone (signed activity ≈ −1.7 and −1.5) yet flips strongly positive in the
three combined contrasts (+3.8, +4.2 and +4.3; interaction FDR 1.5×10⁻³), with both
inference estimators agreeing on the shape. No monotone single dose-response curve
can produce that dip-then-spike. From this one flip the interaction propagates along
two post-transcriptional channels that never touch the steady-state differentially
expressed gene set, which is precisely why it is null on every static gene,
composition and cell-type-specificity axis yet positive on the kinase, causal and
trajectory axes. The first channel is *rate*: the combination supra-additively
accelerates within-state progression along the amyloid-built trajectory (pseudotime
interaction +2.46, loading ≈94% on genuine within-state progression rather than
compartment shifts, with even homeostatic-labelled cells advancing). The second is
*wiring*: the causal reconstruction recovers all five measured interaction
transcription factors from the single GSK3β seed: GSK3β inhibits Mapk14 (p38α),
which normally activates Myc and Foxo3, so the biosynthetic transcription factors
(Myc, Creb1, Tp53, Jun) fall coherently without any change in their mRNA. The
post-translational route is the textbook one: GSK3β phosphorylates Myc at Thr58 to
create the Fbw7 phosphodegron (Welcker et al., 2004), so a kinase flip degrades the
protein while the transcript is unmoved, a mechanism a steady-state expression scan
is structurally blind to.

```{r model_schematic, out.width = "100%", fig.cap = "The lead holistic model (M-UNIFIED), \"amyloid as architect, tau as a rate-and-wiring modifier\". Amyloid (left) templates the homeostatic→DAM activation trajectory and its TREM2/APP-fragment clearance output (top spine); tau acts only on that scaffold, firing a supra-additive GSK3β coincidence (AND-gate) node that drives two post-transcriptional channels: a within-state progression-RATE synergy and a WIRING cascade (GSK3β⊣Mapk14→Myc/Foxo3; the amyloid NF-κB increment attenuated), neither of which rewrites the DAM gene set. Arrowheads = activation, bars = inhibition/suppression, dotted = inferred temporal order, dashed = proposed/off-interaction reaches. The kinase and causal layers are bulk hippocampus, not microglia-sorted. Pre-built out-of-knit by scripts/build_synthesis_model_figure.py."}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(params$figures_dir, "synthesis_model.png"))
```

Several features of the model are deliberate reaches beyond the adjudicated
evidence, and each is flagged. The temporal ordering (amyloid first,
tau-modulation later) is inferred from a single cross-sectional pseudotime axis,
not real time, and is read in the light of the human Aβ→tau inflection point at
which the microglial *transition*, not the static state, separates dementia from
resilience (Lu et al., 2026). The attenuating GSK3β→NF-κB edge is proposed rather
than recovered: the causal layer finds no NF-κB node at the interaction contrast
itself, and the canonical direction of GSK3β on microglial NF-κB is *positive*
(Zhao et al., 2024), so the attenuation reading requires a documented context-switch
rather than default wiring, even though the attenuation itself is real and sealed
(Hyp-1B). The GSK3β node is framed as parenchyma-wide rather than
microglia-exclusive, reconciling the specificity reframe: microglia integrate a
tissue-level signal as accelerated progression while the amyloid main effect stays
microglia-led. Valence is compensatory-leaning (the architect programme is
protective PU.1/MHC-II/TREM2 clearance biology) but genuinely unresolved, because no
senescence panel was measured and the canonical arrest markers move the wrong way
for a clean exhausted terminus. The load-bearing caveat throughout is that the
kinase and causal layers are read from *bulk* hippocampus, not microglia-sorted
tissue, so every GSK3β step is a bulk-signalling-to-microglial-transcription bridge;
only the p38/Mapk14 leg is partially de-risked by the demonstration that p38
activation in P301S is mainly microglial (Perea et al., 2022). The model's chief
epistemic risk is that it can *absorb* its rivals (treating the signalling-only
account as its own second channel, the ceiling account as a substrate partner, and
the exhaustion account as a valence branch), which would make it unfalsifiable by
accretion. Its defence is to make its own *joint* falsifier load-bearing instead: if
microglia-sorted phosphoproteomics shows no within-microglia supra-additive GSK3β
signature *and* an age series shows the progression-rate interaction does not widen
with age, the model collapses to a bulk-tissue ceiling artefact with no
microglia-intrinsic modifier.

## The rivals it must beat

Four rivals sharpen the lead by contesting exactly the inferences it cannot yet
secure; all four respect the sealed verdicts and differ only in how they read the
interaction. The deflationary **saturation-ceiling** model (M-CEIL) is the most
important, because it is the alternative the design openly cannot exclude: an
independent App^NL-G-F^/MAPT^P301S^ cross found the combined microglial response
non-additive and amyloid-dominant (Jiang et al., 2024), and a sub-additive
interaction is what a shared programme near its ceiling would produce. Its premise
is nonetheless strained by the very data it leans on: the matched-power interaction
profiles are *decorrelated* across cell types yet reproducible within one, which a
single shared curve cannot generate, and the GSK3β dip-then-spike cannot come from a
monotone sigmoid. The **compensation-to-exhaustion** model (M-EXH) reads the
interaction as a state-quality collapse in which tau corrupts a protective DAM into a
senescent terminus, a route with strong cell-biological precedent (Brelstaff et al.,
2021; Hu et al., 2021; Graves et al., 2025); it is the weakest of the four here,
because the senescence-specific signal is absent or wrong-signed in the measured data
(the canonical arrest markers move down, p16 is undetected) and the valence axis is
one this design cannot adjudicate. The **tissue-wide convergent-stress** model
(M-TISSUE) relocates the GSK3β core off microglia onto neurons or astrocytes, where
the constitutively active kinase predominantly resides (Perez-Costas et al., 2010),
casting microglia as responders to a parenchymal field; it is honest that the bulk
phospho cannot localise the signal to any compartment, so it is observationally
equivalent to the lead on present evidence and decidable only by cell-type-resolved
measurement. The **state-independent signalling-rewiring** model (M-SIG) is the most
parsimonious: one GSK3β flip and the Thr58 degron explain the nulls and the Myc
suppression together, and the progression-rate synergy is demoted to a downstream
shadow, a single-register claim attractive on Occam grounds but currently untested,
since the decomposition already shows the rate channel is non-redundant with
composition. The comparison is laid out below.

```{r model_comparison, results='asis'}
mc <- rt("synthesis_model_comparison.tsv")
names(mc) <- c("Model", "Core claim", "Amyloid axis", "Tau×amyloid interaction",
               "Key weakness / open flank", "Decisive test")
knitr::kable(
  mc, row.names = FALSE, align = rep("l", 6),
  caption = paste0(
    "Five candidate holistic models compared. The lead (M-UNIFIED) is listed ",
    "first; the four rivals contest only the interaction and all respect the three ",
    "sealed verdicts. 'Decisive test' names the observation that would favour or ",
    "kill each model; the discriminating experiments are detailed below."
  )
)
```

## What would decide between them

Because the models converge on the sealed verdicts and diverge only on the
interaction, the experiments that separate them are well defined, and they run from
a free reanalysis to a multi-year cross. The cheapest is decisive for the
lead-versus-M-SIG question: a within-mouse mediation that conditions the pseudotime
interaction on per-replicate GSK3β/Myc activity (the analogue of the human
mediation already in the pipeline) would show full mediation if the rate channel is
a signalling shadow, or a surviving residual if it is an independent register. The
remaining four are experimental and *extend*, rather than repeat, the controls this
synthesis flags as decisive: a tau-knockout-on-amyloid arm tests whether the GSK3β
AND-gate collapses when tau is removed; microglia-sorted phosphoproteomics tests
whether the GSK3β core is microglia-intrinsic (the lead) or non-microglial
(M-TISSUE), and is the single experiment that lifts the project's largest standing
caveat; a multi-age series tests the temporal ordering and the rate-widening
prediction against the ceiling and exhaustion accounts at once; and a microglial
*Gsk3b* conditional knockout, paired with a senolytic and a neurodegeneration
readout, is the joint arbiter of mechanistic sufficiency and valence. Each row of
the table names which models it separates and how their predictions diverge.

```{r model_experiments, results='asis'}
me <- rt("synthesis_model_discriminating_experiments.tsv")
names(me) <- c("Experiment", "Separates", "Predicted split", "Feasibility")
knitr::kable(
  me, row.names = FALSE, align = rep("l", 4),
  caption = paste0(
    "Experiments that would discriminate the holistic models, cheapest first. The ",
    "first is a free reanalysis of existing caches; the rest extend the controls ",
    "this synthesis flags as decisive (the tau-knockout arm and microglia-sorted ",
    "phosphoproteomics among them)."
  )
)
```

Read as a whole, the generative layer delivers what the contest arithmetic cannot: a
single falsifiable systems model that integrates all eleven arcs and all three
sealed verdicts into one node, the named alternatives it must beat, and an ordered
set of tests, from a free reanalysis to a definitive knockout, that would convert
its provisional lead into a decided one. The lead leads because it is the only
candidate that ties the amyloid architecture, the GSK3β coincidence node, the two
post-transcriptional channels and the clearance output into one mechanism rather
than explaining a single layer; but it leads provisionally, its directional-driver
and microglial-locus claims resting on bulk rather than sorted signalling, its
two-register claim untested in mouse, and its valence genuinely open. That honest,
bounded, testable statement, not a settled mechanism, is the current best holistic
reading the project supports.

# Cross-species translation

The mouse structure is directionally conserved in human Alzheimer cortex, tested
in the SEA-AD single-nucleus atlas of middle-temporal and dorsolateral-prefrontal
cortex (Gabitto et al., 2024) with amyloid indexed by Thal phase and tau by Braak
stage. All four mouse microglial substates resolve in human microglia, and the
disease-emergent supertype occupancy rises monotonically across them (0.150 →
0.490 from homeostatic to proliferative), so the activation gradient is conserved
even though discrete cluster agreement is low. All three pre-registered
interaction mechanisms reproduce their mouse-predicted sign in all five strata
(NF-κB targets negative, the interaction-carrier module negative, and GSK3β targets
positive), and the directional concordance is robust to single-cell refitting
(129 of 130 panel cells) and to an orthogonal AUCell scorer (82 of 90). No
pre-registered interaction survives multiple-testing correction, which is the
expected consequence of amyloid-tau collinearity across human donors (Spearman
≈ 0.65; variance-inflation 1.5-1.9) rather than counter-evidence: a
non-significant interaction at this power is not evidence of absence. The human
microglial-state structure is itself well supported (Sun, Mathys et al., 2023;
Mancuso et al., 2024), and the separable amyloid- and tau-associated profiles
reported in human tissue (Gerrits et al., 2021) match our finding that the
dominant, FDR-surviving human signal is per-pathology main effects rather than an
emergent interaction. The co-escalation of plaque and tangle burden in late-stage
human disease (Gabitto et al., 2024; Lu et al., 2026) *is* the donor-level
collinearity that defeats a factorial interaction term. The right strength of
claim is therefore directional conservation: consistent with the mouse, never a
literal replication, and held in a separate evidence band from the mouse
contests.

# Confidence, caveats and what would change the conclusion

The conclusion is held with graded confidence. The amyloid-driven DAM programme
is the most secure element (multi-modal, multi-method, power-robust and
externally canonical), though its protective-versus-pathogenic valence is
unresolved by these data and the literature leans protective. The interaction
mechanism (GSK3β up, Myc down, wired and dynamics-positive) is internally coherent
and externally plausible but rests on activity inference rather than direct
gene-level DE, and three caveats temper it. The phosphoproteome is bulk
hippocampus, not microglia-sorted, so every kinase and causal-topology result is a
bulk-signalling-to-microglial-transcription bridge rather than a within-microglia
measurement. The gene-level dynamics signature is roughly one-quarter ambient and
ribosomal contamination and names neither GSK3β nor Myc. And the whole synthesis is
a single mouse cohort at one 24-month timepoint, with sex perfectly aliased to
batch. The NF-κB attenuation has a ceiling/saturation alternative the design
cannot exclude, and the synaptic verdict is clearance-dominant rather than
complement-absent.

Three specific experiments would move a verdict rather than add orthogonal
corroboration at diminishing return. A tau-knockout-on-amyloid arm
(MAPT-null × App^NL-G-F^) would convert the tau-as-modifier claims from
genotype-correlational to causal, and is the single most informative missing
control. Microglia-sorted phosphoproteomics would lift the GSK3β interaction
result out of its bulk-tissue caveat and test whether the kinase synergy is
microglia-intrinsic. And a powered, amyloid-tau-decoupling human cohort such as
ROSMAP, currently deferred for want of a data-use agreement, would test whether
the directionally-conserved human interaction survives once the donor-level
collinearity that defeats it here is broken. Absent these, the project has reached
the point where further evidence arcs would corroborate the existing reading
rather than overturn it.

# Conclusion {-}

The best current conclusion is that amyloid is the primary driver of microglial
remodelling in this 2×2 model, mobilising a robust and largely compensatory DAM
programme, while tau acts as a modifier that does not rewrite the
amyloid-determined gene set but instead bends the response in three specific ways:
it supra-additively engages a GSK3β-kinase and Myc-suppression signalling module
at the tau×amyloid interface, it accelerates the rate at which amyloid pushes
microglia toward the DAM state, and it attenuates the amyloid-driven NF-κB
transcriptional response. The downstream synaptic remodelling is carried by
TREM2/APP-fragment clearance signalling rather than classical complement. The
divergence the project set out to find is real but does not live where a
differential-expression scan would look for it: it is a signalling and dynamic
phenomenon, tissue-wide rather than microglia-restricted, and directionally
conserved in human Alzheimer cortex.

# References {-}

Bhaskar K, Konerth M, Kokiko-Cochran ON, Cardona A, Ransohoff RM, Lamb BT (2010).
Regulation of tau pathology by the microglial fractalkine receptor. *Neuron*
68(1):19-31.

Brelstaff JH, Mason M, Katsinelos T, et al. (2021). Microglia become
hypofunctional and release metalloproteases and tau seeds when phagocytosing live
neurons with P301S tau aggregates. *Science Advances* 7(43):eabg4980.

Bryan KJ, Zhu X, Harris PL, et al. (2009). Expression of CD74 is increased in
neurofibrillary tangles in Alzheimer's disease and interacts with APP to suppress
Aβ production. *Molecular Neurodegeneration* 4:41.

Chen Y, Colonna M (2021). Microglia in Alzheimer's disease at single-cell level.
Are there common patterns in humans and mice? *Journal of Experimental Medicine*
218(9):e20202717.

Coburn JM, et al. (2025). Amyloid and tau pathologies synergistically drive
human microglial responses in xenografted models. *Alzheimer's & Dementia*.

Deczkowska A, Keren-Shaul H, Weiner A, Colonna M, Schwartz M, Amit I (2018).
Disease-associated microglia: a universal immune sensor of neurodegeneration.
*Cell* 173(5):1073-1081.

Dejanovic B, Huntley MA, De Mazière A, et al. (2018). Changes in the synaptic
proteome in tauopathy and rescue of tau-induced synapse loss by C1q antibodies.
*Neuron* 100(6):1322-1336.

Gabitto MI, Travaglini KJ, Rachleff VM, et al. (2024). Integrated multimodal
cell atlas of Alzheimer's disease (SEA-AD). *Nature Neuroscience* 27:2366-2383.

Gerrits E, Brouwer N, Kooistra SM, et al. (2021). Distinct amyloid-β and tau
associated microglia profiles in Alzheimer's disease. *Acta Neuropathologica*
141(5):681-696.

Graves et al. (2025). p16-expressing microglia and endothelial cells promote
tauopathy and neurovascular abnormalities in PS19 mice. *Neuron*.

Hong S, Beja-Glasser VF, Nfonoyim BM, et al. (2016). Complement and microglia
mediate early synapse loss in Alzheimer mouse models. *Science*
352(6286):712-716.

Hooper C, Killick R, Lovestone S (2008). The GSK3 hypothesis of Alzheimer's
disease. *Journal of Neurochemistry* 104(6):1433-1439.

Hu Y, et al. (2021). Replicative senescence dictates the emergence of
disease-associated microglia and contributes to Aβ pathology. *Cell Reports*
35(10):109228.

Huang KL, Marcora E, Pimenova AA, et al. (2017). A common haplotype lowers PU.1
expression in myeloid cells and delays onset of Alzheimer's disease. *Nature
Neuroscience* 20(8):1052-1061.

Ising C, Venegas C, Zhang S, et al. (2019). NLRP3 inflammasome activation drives
tau pathology. *Nature* 575(7784):669-673.

Jiang L, et al. (2024). β-amyloid accumulation enhances microtubule-associated
protein tau pathology in an App^NL-G-F^/MAPT^P301S^ mouse model. *Frontiers in
Neuroscience* 18:1372297.

Keren-Shaul H, Spinrad A, Weiner A, et al. (2017). A unique microglia type
associated with restricting development of Alzheimer's disease. *Cell*
169(7):1276-1290.

Kim B, Kim H, Jeong H, et al. (2022). Amyloid-β and tau pathologies act
synergistically to induce novel disease stage-specific microglia subtypes.
*Molecular Neurodegeneration* 17:83.

Krasemann S, Madore C, Cialic R, et al. (2017). The TREM2-APOE pathway drives the
transcriptional phenotype of dysfunctional microglia in neurodegenerative
diseases. *Immunity* 47(3):566-581.

Lauretti E, Dincer O, Praticò D (2020). Glycogen synthase kinase-3 signaling in
Alzheimer's disease. *Biochimica et Biophysica Acta - Molecular Cell Research*
1867(5):118664.

Lee SH, Meilandt WJ, Xie L, et al. (2021). Trem2 restrains the enhancement of tau
accumulation and neurodegeneration by β-amyloid pathology. *Neuron*
104(7):1283-1297.

Lu et al. (2026). Human microglial transitions at the Aβ-tau inflection point
associate with divergent pathways to dementia and resilience. *Nature Medicine*.

Mancuso R, Fattorelli N, Martinez-Muriana A, et al. (2024). A multi-pathology
human microglial xenotransplantation model reveals a TREM2- and APOE-modulated
HLA response to amyloid. *Nature Neuroscience* 27:886-900.

Maphis N, Xu G, Kokiko-Cochran ON, et al. (2015). Reactive microglia drive tau
pathology and contribute to the spreading of pathological tau in the brain.
*Brain* 138(6):1738-1755.

Perea JR, et al. (2022). p38 activation occurs mainly in microglia in the P301S
Tauopathy mouse model. *Scientific Reports* 12:2130.

Perez-Costas E, Gandy JC, Meléndez-Ferro M, Roberts RC, Bijur GN (2010). Light and
electron microscopy study of glycogen synthase kinase-3β in the mouse brain. *PLoS
ONE* 5(1):e8911.

Pickett EK, Herrmann AG, McQueen J, et al. (2019). Amyloid beta and tau cooperate
to cause reversible behavioral and transcriptional deficits in a model of
Alzheimer's disease. *Cell Reports* 29(11):3592-3604.

Saito T, Matsuba Y, Mihira N, et al. (2014). Single App knock-in mouse models of
Alzheimer's disease. *Nature Neuroscience* 17(5):661-663.

Squair JW, Gautier M, Kathe C, et al. (2021). Confronting false discoveries in
single-cell differential expression. *Nature Communications* 12:5692.

Sun N, Victor MB, Park YP, et al. (2023). Human microglial state dynamics in
Alzheimer's disease progression. *Cell* 186(20):4386-4403.

Wang C, et al. (Cell Discov. 2022). Distinct phases of adult microglia
proliferation: a Myc-mediated early phase and a Tnfaip3-mediated terminal phase.
*Cell Discovery* 8:130.

Wang C, Fan L, Khawaja RR, et al. (Nat. Commun. 2022). Microglial NF-κB drives
tau spreading and toxicity in a mouse model of tauopathy. *Nature Communications*
13:1969.

Welcker M, Orian A, Jin J, et al. (2004). The Fbw7 tumor suppressor regulates
glycogen synthase kinase 3 phosphorylation-dependent c-Myc protein degradation.
*PNAS* 101(24):9085-9090.

Yun H, et al. (2026). Microglial SPI1/PU.1 is required for the protective
response to amyloid pathology. *Neuron*.

Zhao Y, et al. (2024). GSK3: a potential target and pending issues for treatment
of Alzheimer's disease. *CNS Neuroscience & Therapeutics* 30(7):e14818.

Zhong L, Sheng X, Wang W, et al. (2023). TREM2 receptor protects against
complement-mediated synaptic loss by binding to complement C1q during
neurodegeneration. *Immunity* 56(8):1794-1808.

# Reproducibility {-}

This synthesis is read-only: it re-reads the exported result tables in
`storage/results/` and embeds the pre-built capstone figure, recomputing nothing
and leaving the locked `analysis.Rmd` section-17 adjudication untouched.

```{r session_info}
sessionInfo()
```
